# Help text is what humans and LLMs read first — pin the headline
# parts so a refactor that removes them is caught.

exec eon --help
stdout 'cron-style'
stdout 'add '
stdout 'ls '
stdout 'logs '
stdout 'install'
# daemon is hidden — the supervisor unit is the supported entrypoint.
! stdout '^\s+daemon\s'

# `eon add --help` must enumerate the cron + at syntax in enough
# detail to write a valid expression without external docs. We pin
# the section headers and several representative tokens.
exec eon add --help
stdout 'CRON SYNTAX'
stdout 'MINUTE HOUR DAY-OF-MONTH MONTH DAY-OF-WEEK'
stdout '0-59'
stdout '0-23'
stdout 'jan,feb,mar'
stdout 'sun,mon,tue'
stdout 'A,B,C'
stdout '\*/N'
stdout 'A-B/N'
stdout '@yearly'
stdout '@monthly'
stdout '@weekly'
stdout '@daily'
stdout '@hourly'
stdout '@every DURATION'
stdout 'NOT supported'
stdout '@reboot'
stdout 'ONE-SHOT TIME'
stdout 'RFC3339'
stdout 'Relative offset'
stdout '\+30s'
stdout '\+3d'
stdout 'today 17:00'
stdout 'tomorrow 9am'

exec eon logs --help
stdout '--follow'
stdout '--lines'
stdout '--since'
stdout 'Behaviour matrix'

exec eon ls --help
stdout '--kind'
stdout '--status'
stdout '--json'

# daemon is hidden but invocable; --help still works for the supervisor.
exec eon daemon --help
stdout 'Internal entrypoint'
stdout 'SIGHUP'
